questions.prompt: Your team spans 4 countries. Meeting times always favor one region. What is the best first action?
options: Keep the same schedule for consistency.
Rotate meeting times and share async notes.
Ask one region to always attend late.
Cancel live meetings permanently.
questions.rationale: Rotation and async documentation improve fairness and participation.

questions.prompt: A teammate struggles with an idiom-heavy instruction message. What should you do?
options: Repeat the same message more loudly.
Use plain language and confirm understanding.
Ignore the issue to save time.
Replace text with slang examples.
questions.rationale: Clear language and confirmation reduce communication barriers.

questions.prompt: An applicant's portfolio is excellent, but internet outages caused a late submission in a low-connectivity region. Best policy-consistent response?
options: Auto-reject late submissions without review.
Apply a documented exception process with evidence checks.
Approve all late submissions automatically.
Ignore submission rules entirely.
questions.rationale: A transparent exception policy balances fairness and standards.

questions.prompt: You discover a critical bug in production on a Friday evening. Your team lead is offline and the on-call engineer is in a different time zone. What do you do?
options: Wait until Monday to report it.
Fix it yourself and push directly to production without review.
Document the issue, apply a safe rollback or hotfix, and notify the on-call engineer.
Post about it on social media to get attention.
Ignore it since it's not your on-call shift.
questions.rationale: Documentation + safe rollback + alerting on-call balances urgency with process.

questions.prompt: When giving code review feedback to a teammate from a different cultural background, it's better to be as blunt and direct as possible regardless of cultural norms.
options: True
False
questions.rationale: Effective communication adjusts for cultural context while remaining honest. Bluntness without awareness can harm collaboration.

questions.prompt: You're mentoring a junior developer who keeps making the same mistake. You've explained the concept three times. What's your best approach?
options: Explain it a fourth time with the same approach.
Do the work for them to save time.
Try a different teaching method — pair program, draw a diagram, or have them explain it back to you.
Report them to management.
Tell them to figure it out on their own.
questions.rationale: Different people learn differently. Switching methods (visual, hands-on, teaching back) often breaks through understanding barriers.
